data.title: Trump team reportedly exploring implications of oil reaching $200 per barrel
data.description:
Trump administration officials are examining what a potential spike in oil prices as high as $200 a barrel would mean for the economy, a sign senior officials are studying the possible fallout from extreme scenarios for the Iran war, according to a report by Bloomberg News.

Modeling of how damaging a bigger jump in oil prices could be to growth prospects is part of regular assessment done during times of strain and is not a prediction, according to the people, who asked not to be identified commenting on matters that aren’t public.

The effort is aimed at making sure the administration is prepared for all contingencies, including a prolonged conflict, they said.

The latest comments by Iran suggested some willingness by Tehran to negotiate an end to the war if ​its demands were met. The U.S. sent a 15-point ceasefire proposal to Iran that was originally brushed aside by Iranian officials.

WTI crude (CL1:COM) futures were up 1.6% to $91.71/bbl at press time, Brent (CO1:COM) also gained 1.5% to $103.78/bbl, and front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for April delivery ticked up 0.5% to $2.97/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Thursday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time. 

Claims seen at 210K, closer to the 4-week moving average of 210.75K, up from 205K last week.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report 

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country.

11:00 AM Kansas City Fed Manufacturing Index

The Kansas City Fed index offers a monthly assessment of change in the region's manufacturing sector. 

1:00 PM 7-Yr Note Auction

Treasury notes are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury note issue.

4:00 PM Lisa Cook Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Lisa Cook speaks on "Reflections on Financial Stability" before an event co-presented by the Yale Program on Financial Stability.

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet 

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

6:30 PM Stephen Miran Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran speaks on "The Fed's Balance Sheet" before the Economic Club of Miami.

7:00 PM Philip Jefferson Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Philip Jefferson speaks on the economic outlook and energy effects, and Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Lorie Logan is moderator before the Global Perspectives Speaker Series.

7:10 PM Michael Barr Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr speaks on the economy before an event hosted by the Brookings Institution.

data.title: Karman Holdings expects 53% revenue growth for 2026 while expanding capacity and M&A activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Karman Holdings Inc. (KRMN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Karman welcomed new CEO Jonathan Rambeau, who shared his enthusiasm for leading the company, citing Karman's "deep engineering expertise and vertically integrated full-spectrum manufacturing capabilities position the company as a unique enabler for national security and the growing space economy." (Chief Executive Officer Jonathan Rambeau)
  • Outgoing CEO Anthony Koblinski highlighted record performance in the fourth quarter and full year: "Our team delivered another quarter of record performance, driven by outstanding execution across the business and strong momentum following our February 2025 IPO... key fourth quarter highlights include: record quarterly revenue of $134 million, with growth across all three end markets. Record gross profit of $54 million. Adjusted EBITDA of $42 million, another quarterly record for Karman, and backlog reached an all-time high of $801 million." (Chief Executive Officer Anthony Koblinski)
  • Koblinski underlined the strategic expansion through acquisitions: "During 2025, we completed three acquisitions: MTI, ISP and Five Axis... In January, we further expanded our platform with the acquisition of Seemann and MSC, extending our reach into Maritime Defense... capabilities we will leverage across the entire Karman portfolio." (Chief Executive Officer Anthony Koblinski)
  • CFO Michael Willis reported: "Revenue of $134 million, represented a 47% increase compared to fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024. Gross profit grew 54% to $54 million, increasing gross profit margin at 40%. Net income rose over 300% to $8 million. Adjusted EBITDA jumped to $42 million, a 59% year-over-year increase. Adjusted EPS more than tripled to $0.11 per diluted share from $0.03, and backlog grew 38% year-over-year to $801 million." (Chief Financial Officer Michael Willis)
  • COO Jonathan Beaudoin discussed capacity expansion: "Tony mentioned our plans for a new Salt Lake City manufacturing hub, which will add nearly 200,000 square feet, quadruple our production capacity for loitering UAV launch systems and add valuable redundant nozzle manufacturing capacity."

Outlook

  • CEO Jonathan Rambeau provided 2026 guidance: "We now expect full year revenue of $715 million to $730 million and non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA of $207 million to $218 million. This represents 53% year-over-year revenue and 46% adjusted EBITDA growth, an additional growth above our previously communicated 2026 guidance given this past January."
  • Rambeau noted that "revenue growth to be roughly split between organic and inorganic" and that first half revenue and adjusted EBITDA will represent about 45% of the annual total, with "sequential quarterly growth similar to that of last year."
  • The demand environment is described as favorable, with "strong market conditions and the Seemann and MSC acquisition expanded our backlog to more than $1 billion, providing approximately 80% visibility to the midpoint of our full year revenue guidance range as of March 20, 2026."

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue reached $134 million, with gross profit of $54 million and adjusted EBITDA of $42 million. Net income was $8 million and adjusted EPS was $0.11.
  • For the full year, revenue was $472 million, gross profit $190 million (40% margin), net income $17 million, adjusted EBITDA $145 million, and adjusted EPS $0.37.
  • Backlog at year-end was $801 million, up 38% year-over-year.
  • End market mix in Q4: Space and Launch 27%, Hypersonics and SMD 36%, Tactical Missiles and IDS 37%. For the full year: Space and Launch 32%, Hypersonics and SMD 32%, Tactical Missiles and IDS 36%.
  • The company ended Q4 with $34 million in cash and equivalents, invested $20 million in CapEx, and total debt increased to $768 million following acquisitions.
  • Karman increased its revolving credit facility to $150 million and expects CapEx to be 5% of 2026 revenue, or about $36 million.

Q&A

  • Peter Arment, Baird: Asked about multiyear frameworks and missile production ramp-up. CEO Jonathan Rambeau responded that Karman "will benefit from the outcomes of the frameworks," but doesn't expect related orders until the fourth quarter of this year or later.
  • Kenneth Herbert, RBC: Inquired about backlog margin mix and pricing pressures. CEO Rambeau stated "there's really no notable mix changes... it's pretty steady course," but noted Seemann's cost-plus contracts may affect near-term margins.
  • Clarke Jeffries, Piper Sandler: Questioned whether recent changes affected investment plans. CFO Michael Willis said "no shift in terms of our priorities, just gives us more conviction to lean into the investments we already had planned."
  • John Godyn, Citigroup: Asked about supply chain risks and Golden Dome timing. CEO Rambeau described supply chain risk as "low" and said Golden Dome could drive upside orders in late 2026 or 2027.
  • Louie Dipalma, William Blair: Sought insights on space business trends and defense prime outsourcing. CEO Rambeau affirmed opportunities for Karman to support primes and expand market share.
  • Alexandra Mandery, Truist: Asked about contract delays and low-cost munitions production. CEO Koblinski said delays are "just a timing matter rather than will the orders come through."
  • Austin Bohlig, Needham: Inquired about potential upside from congressional funding. CEO Rambeau said any upside would likely materialize as orders in late 2026, with volume in 2027.
  • Michael Leshock, KeyBanc: Sought detail on NASA Ignition program and hypersonics growth. COO Beaudoin emphasized Karman's expanded clean room and ongoing hypersonics participation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on order delays, supply chain risk, margin composition, and the timing of anticipated market upswings, but the tone was constructive, focusing on growth opportunities and execution.
  • Management's tone was consistently confident, with CEO Rambeau stating "we remain confident in our long-term outlook of strong organic growth, supplemented by strategic accretive acquisitions."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts expressed increased conviction, especially regarding the favorable demand environment and integration of acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue, gross profit, adjusted EBITDA, and backlog all reached new records, rising from Q3 to Q4.
  • Guidance for 2026 increased both in revenue and EBITDA, moving from a previously discussed 20% to 25% growth in 2026 to a new expectation of 53% revenue and 46% EBITDA growth, driven by both organic and inorganic contributions.
  • The addition of Maritime Defense Systems as a fourth reporting segment marks a strategic broadening of end markets.
  • Management's tone in Q4 emphasized capacity expansion and readiness for future demand, with greater detail on integration strategies and investment plans.
  • Analysts continued to focus on order timing, supply chain, and M&A pipeline, but showed confidence in the durability of Karman's growth trajectory.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a temporary slowdown in contracting activity due to the federal government shutdown, affecting Q4 2025 and Q1 2026.
  • Delays in order timing and Golden Dome program ramp-up were discussed, though management expressed confidence these are timing-related, not lost opportunities.
  • Supply chain risks were described as low, with proactive steps taken through acquisitions and long-term supplier engagement.
  • Margin headwinds related to the integration of cost-plus contracts from Seemann and MSC were included in 2026 guidance.

Final Takeaway

Karman Holdings closed 2025 with record financial performance and a robust backlog, underpinned by successful execution and a series of strategic acquisitions. The company projects a 53% increase in revenue for 2026, with a balanced split between organic and inorganic growth. Proactive investments in capacity and technology, a new CEO at the helm, and ongoing integration of recent acquisitions position Karman to address strong demand across all end markets, while management remains confident in the sustainability of its growth trajectory and its ability to navigate timing-related challenges in contracting and funding.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Journey Medical targets expanded Emrosi coverage and EBITDA growth through 2026 amid robust prescription demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Journey Medical Corporation (DERM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Claude Maraoui, Founder, President, CEO & Director, described 2025 as a milestone year, highlighting the successful launch of Emrosi, Journey’s internally developed oral rosacea treatment. "Emrosi was made available to pharmacies in late March of last year, and our promotional activities began in early April. I am pleased to report that during the 3 quarters of 2025 in which Emrosi was commercially available, the product achieved $14.7 million in net sales."
  • Maraoui reported, "We delivered total net product revenue growth of 11%, and we improved our gross margin by nearly 3.5 percentage points compared to the 2024 period. Importantly, our business was able to make solid financial progress despite pressure on our Accutane franchise and other legacy products due to generic competition."
  • The company recorded positive adjusted EBITDA as well as positive EBITDA in Q4 2025 and expects to remain adjusted EBITDA positive in 2026 and beyond. Maraoui stated, "With our solid cash position of approximately $24 million, I believe that Journey is well positioned to execute on our business plan and grow sales and profitability with the resources that we have in place."
  • Prescription volume for Emrosi grew nearly 50% sequentially in Q4 versus Q3, with over 3,500 unique dermatology prescribers having written at least one script for Emrosi by year end.
  • Maraoui noted, "At present, approximately 100 million commercial covered lives have access to Emrosi. This includes contracts in place with 2 of the top 3 group purchasing organizations in the United States."
  • Plans to launch one or two incremental dermatology products later in 2026 were announced.
  • Joseph Benesch, CFO, stated, "Total revenues for the year were $61.9 million, representing a 10% increase compared to $56.1 million for 2024. The increase reflects incremental net product revenue related to the successful U.S. commercial launch of Emrosi."

Outlook

  • Management expects to remain adjusted EBITDA positive in 2026 and for the foreseeable future. "As coverage expands and formulary policies mature throughout 2026, we expect to see improved reimbursement rates, reduced reliance on our co-pay bridging program and an increase in Emrosi sales and overall operating margin expansion," Maraoui explained.
  • Guidance for more detailed financial metrics will be provided later in the year as visibility on downstream health plan adoption improves.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $61.9 million in total revenues for 2025, with gross margin at 66.2%. The year ended with $24.1 million in cash and $29.4 million in working capital.
  • GAAP net loss narrowed to $11.4 million, or $0.47 per share, compared to a loss of $14.7 million, or $0.72 per share, in 2024.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was a positive $2.9 million for 2025, up from $800,000 in 2024. EBITDA improved by $5.2 million year-over-year.
  • SG&A expenses totaled $44.4 million, reflecting increased investment to support Emrosi’s launch and expansion.

Q&A

  • Scott Henry, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about Q1 prescription trends and the impact of insurance deductible resets and weather. CEO Maraoui responded that March prescriptions have rebounded and expects Q1 prescriptions to surpass Q4 numbers, stating, "We haven't lost any steam at all, and we continue to expect substantial growth with Emrosi moving forward."
  • Henry also asked about sequential momentum. Maraoui indicated, "You're going to see a nice build and momentum with this...we certainly expect to be well past that overall."
  • Henry questioned revenue per script volatility. Maraoui attributed Q4’s lower figure to reimbursement mix and expects upward pressure in 2026 as reimbursement improves; new GPO contracts and the lifting of new-to-market moratoriums should benefit future revenue per script.
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley Securities: Asked about gross to net expectations. COO Ramsey Alloush explained, "We expect improvement throughout 2026, gradual quarter-over-quarter...as we hit certain milestones with national formularies and getting adoption for Emrosi to become a covered drug on formulary, we expect, again, incremental gain in terms of gross to net."
  • Mamtani inquired about upcoming product launches and marketing spend. Maraoui indicated one or two launches likely in the second half of 2026, with expenses already included in the budget and no distraction from Emrosi as the core focus.
  • Brandon Folkes, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about inventory and revenue per script trajectory. Maraoui said inventory is on track with demand and that average gross to net for 2025 was [280], within internal expectations, with upward pressure expected due to improved reimbursement.
  • Thomas Flaten, Lake Street Capital: Inquired about accounts receivable and margin outlook. CFO Benesch said the increase was due to timing and expects continued gross margin improvement as high-margin products become a larger revenue share.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on prescription momentum, revenue per script, and gross to net outcomes, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged strong prescription trends but probed for sustainability and margin details.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strong execution, market traction, and anticipated improvements: "We are planning to increase our sales force...and that should be ramped up no later than the first part of Q3," Maraoui said, signaling proactive growth efforts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from optimistic about early traction to confident in sustaining and expanding profitability and margin improvements, while analysts maintained a pragmatic approach, seeking more granularity on financial metrics and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Emrosi’s prescription volume surged sequentially in Q4, with the unique prescriber base growing from over 2,700 in Q3 to 3,500 by year-end.
  • Revenue growth slowed from 21% in Q3 to 10% for the full year, but gross margin improved by nearly 3.5 percentage points year-over-year, versus sequential improvement in prior quarters.
  • Management shifted from focusing on early adoption and payer contracting in Q3 to emphasizing sustained profitability and margin expansion in Q4.
  • Analysts continued to focus on revenue per script, gross to net, and payer dynamics, reflecting ongoing concern about conversion of prescription growth into sustained revenue and margin gains.
  • Management outlined clear plans for new product launches and incremental sales force expansion in 2026, signaling an ongoing focus on scaling operations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competitive pressure on the Accutane franchise and other legacy products due to generics.
  • The conversion of prescription demand into revenue remains dependent on the pace of health plan coverage adoption and reimbursement improvements.
  • Reliance on co-pay bridging programs continues until broader payer coverage is implemented, which could impact net revenue per script in the near term.
  • Analysts raised concerns about gross to net improvement timing, inventory levels, and accounts receivable timing, which management addressed as being on track or due to operational timing.

Final Takeaway

Journey Medical Corporation’s 2025 results underscore the successful commercial launch of Emrosi, with strong prescription demand, increasing prescriber adoption, and improving margins. Management expects continued EBITDA positivity, margin expansion, and sales growth in 2026 as health plan coverage matures, reimbursement improves, and the company leverages its established sales infrastructure for additional product launches. The company maintains a solid cash position and is focused on disciplined expense management while navigating ongoing challenges in legacy product lines and payer dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Modiv Industrial outlines accelerated asset recycling, targets 100% manufacturing portfolio within 24 months
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Modiv Industrial (MDV) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Aaron Halfacre opened the call by acknowledging a transition in the finance leadership, noting that "this is going to be Ray's last earnings call, even though Ray is going to be with us for the remainder of the year, it's his last official earnings call, and John is going to be taking over the helm." He emphasized gratitude for the accounting and finance team, stating their efficiency allows him to focus on strategic, forward-thinking initiatives.

  • CFO Raymond Pacini reported, "Rental income for the fourth quarter was $11 million compared with $11.7 million in the prior year period." He explained that the decrease was primarily due to the expiration of leases on two office properties, one of which was sold in December and the other is pending sale.

  • Pacini added, "Fourth quarter adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, was $4 million compared to $4.1 million in the year ago quarter." AFFO per share fell to $0.32 from $0.37 in the prior year, attributed to an increase in diluted shares outstanding from share issuances and partnership units.

  • Pacini detailed that, as of December 31, 2025, "total cash and cash equivalents were $14.4 million, and we had $30 million available to draw on our revolver," and that all consolidated debt is now at a fixed interest rate of 4.15% with no maturities until July 2028.

Outlook

  • Halfacre indicated that asset recycling will "start to pick up in earnest" in 2026, particularly focusing on noncore office assets. He explained that current market volatility, especially interest rate fluctuations, is affecting transaction pipelines and buyer confidence, but the company remains committed to its recycling strategy.

  • The CEO elaborated that the goal is to transition to "a rock-solid portfolio long term" by selling office and noncore assets and reallocating into manufacturing-focused industrial properties. He stated, "We will continue to honor our recycling. I think the way we're thinking about the recycling...the first phase is really looking at like we have some noncore assets, particularly office. Those are going to get -- we're going to get rid of those, right?"

  • On the timeline for achieving a pure-play manufacturing portfolio, Halfacre stated, "So yes, I do think 24 months is very realistic and doable."

Financial Results

  • Pacini reported a $30,000 year-over-year decrease in AFFO, with a $554,000 drop in cash rents partially offset by reductions in interest expense, preferred stock dividends, property expenses, and G&A. Interest expense rose by $1.1 million from the prior year, attributed to amortization of off-market interest rate swaps.

  • As of quarter-end, Modiv Industrial reported $262.1 million in consolidated debt, with $250 million outstanding on its $280 million credit facility. The company highlighted that it has no debt maturities until July 2028 following a credit facility extension in January 2026.

Q&A

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners, inquired about offers received and the decision not to pursue one. Halfacre responded, "We, at that moment, didn't see a secure path forward. So we stepped back from discussions...our job is to protect our investors and to make sure that we have put forward the requests that we need."

  • Mehta asked about asset sales and acquisitions for 2026. Halfacre explained the multi-phase recycling plan, focusing initially on divesting noncore office assets and a large Kia dealership, and then evaluating shorter WALT properties for sale or extension. He noted that "the interest has been above the appraised value of the property" for the San Diego office but emphasized timing and tax sensitivity in transactions.

  • Jay Kornreich, Cantor Fitzgerald, questioned the timeline for achieving a 100% manufacturing portfolio. Halfacre said, "that 24 months, I think if things are rosy and the market starts hitting its stride, that's a 12-month process, right?...the bottleneck is having the right assets to acquire."

  • John Massocca, B. Riley Securities, asked if inbound acquisition interest might accelerate a strategic alternatives process. Halfacre replied, "what this suggests to me is that barring someone closing that value gap -- and again, closing that value gap does not mean $22...But there's no upside, right? They don't -- they want it bad. They just buy a bond, right? So they need upside, but our investors need upside."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions focused on transaction strategy, asset sales, market volatility, and inbound M&A interest, with a probing but neutral tone aimed at clarifying management's approach to value creation and risk management.

  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic and deliberate tone throughout, emphasizing patience in asset sales and acquisitions. Halfacre used phrases such as "we're going to be thoughtful about the timing" and "we're just being extremely thoughtful" when discussing strategic moves.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained consistent—focused on clarity and process—while management's tone shifted from gritty optimism to a more measured, patient approach in light of ongoing market volatility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured an explicit commitment to accelerate asset recycling and a clear timeline to achieve a 100% manufacturing-focused portfolio within 24 months, compared to the prior quarter's more general optimism and discussion of market volatility.

  • Management's strategic focus has shifted from waiting for market conditions to improve, to actively cleaning up the portfolio and seizing recycling opportunities as they arise.

  • Analysts' questions in both quarters centered on pipeline visibility, cap rates, and asset sales, but in Q4 there was increased interest in the company's attractiveness as an M&A target.

  • Financial results revealed declining rental income and AFFO per share due to asset dispositions and increased share count, but also highlighted improvements in liquidity and debt structure.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged ongoing market volatility, particularly fluctuating interest rates, as a challenge for both acquisitions and dispositions, stating, "you've got rates just gyrating all over, and that just really stings confidence for buyers and sellers in general."

  • The company identified tax sensitivity as a key consideration in asset sales, especially for properties with low tax basis and the need for 1031 exchanges to avoid taxable events.

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the impact of asset sales on recurring income and the timing of replacement acquisitions, as well as the risk of failing to close the value gap in potential M&A situations.

Final Takeaway

Modiv Industrial is advancing an accelerated asset recycling strategy with a focus on divesting noncore office and select industrial assets over the next 24 months to achieve a pure-play manufacturing portfolio. Management highlighted improved liquidity, a fixed-rate debt structure with no maturities until 2028, and a disciplined approach to maximizing shareholder value. Despite market volatility and headwinds from rising interest expense, the company remains patient, tax-aware, and committed to extracting maximum value from each transaction while positioning itself for potential M&A interest and long-term portfolio strength.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Woodside assumes control of Beaumont plant, delays lower-carbon ammonia production
data.description:
Woodside Energy (WDS) said Wednesday it assumed operational control of the Beaumont New Ammonia facility in Texas after completing ​performance testing and handover from OCI ​Global.

The company said the plant has the capacity to produce and ⁠export up to 1.1M metric tons/year ​of ammonia, adding diversity to its portfolio as it expands into new energy products.

Woodside (WDS) said ammonia production at the plant ​began in December, but production of ​lower-carbon ammonia is expected to be delayed until after 2026 due ‌to ⁠construction issues at a third-party feedstock supply facility; the company had planned to begin producing lower-carbon ammonia in the U.S. in this year's H2.

Woodside (WDS) acquired the Beaumont project in September 2024 for US$2.35B; the facility achieved a milestone in December, when it produced traditional, or gray, ammonia for the first time.

data.title: Celcuity outlines potential $2.5B annual peak revenue for gedatolisib amid pivotal clinical milestones and commercial launch preparations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Celcuity Inc. (CELC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Sullivan highlighted that 2025 was pivotal for Celcuity, stating the company is preparing for "the potential approval and commercialization of gedatolisib." He reviewed several milestones: "the FDA accepted our new drug application...granted it priority review with the Prescription Drug User Fee Act or PDUFA goal date of July 17, 2026," and noted the NDA was under the real-time oncology review program. Sullivan cited "unprecedented efficacy data from the PIK3CA wild-type cohort of the Phase III VIKTORIA-1 clinical trial," and expects reporting of the PIK3CA mutant cohort results in Q2 2026.

  • Sullivan also detailed late-breaking data presented at major oncology conferences and a recent peer-reviewed publication, emphasizing new benchmarks in progression-free survival (PFS) and duration of response for gedatolisib in HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. He recapped: "median progression-free survival...for the gedatolisib triplet...was 9.3 months compared to only 2 months for fulvestrant and the hazard ratio was 0.24."

  • Management discussed safety, noting "gedatolisib triplet was generally well tolerated...with mostly low-grade adverse events." Sullivan highlighted minimal impact on glucose levels, which distinguishes gedatolisib from other PI3K-alpha inhibitors.

  • Commercial preparations are advancing, with Sullivan stating, "we've since largely completed building the organization, including our sales force and internal systems required to operate as a commercial stage company." He estimated a "total addressable market for gedatolisib in the second-line setting is more than $5 billion," with potential to "generate peak revenue of up to $2.5 billion annually."

  • CFO Vicky Hahne reported, "our fourth quarter net loss was $51 million or $0.97 per share compared to $36.7 million net loss, or $0.85 per share, for the fourth quarter of 2024." Hahne added, "cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments were $441.5 million at the end of fiscal year 2025 and are expected to finance our operations through 2027."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated expectations for a "potential approval and commercialization of gedatolisib" with an FDA PDUFA goal date of July 17, 2026.

  • Sullivan projected reporting of the PIK3CA mutant cohort results from the VIKTORIA-1 trial in Q2 2026, with full results to be presented at a medical conference in 2026.

  • The company aims for "peak revenue of up to $2.5 billion annually" for a second-line indication if positive results are reported for both wild-type and mutant cohorts.

Financial Results

  • Hahne reported, "net loss for the full year was $177 million, or $3.79 per share, compared to $111.8 million, or $2.83 per share, compared to the same period in 2024."

  • Non-GAAP adjusted net loss for Q4 2025 was $38.4 million, or $0.73 per share. Research and development expenses for Q4 were $37.6 million, with a $4.1 million increase attributed to "increased employee and consulting expenses, of which $5.3 million related to commercial headcount additions and other launch-related activities."

  • General and administrative expenses for Q4 2025 were $11.6 million, up $8.6 million from the prior year period, driven largely by "increased employee and consulting expenses, of which $5.4 million related to noncash stock-based compensation."

  • Cash used in operating activities for Q4 2025 was $36.4 million, and for the year totaled $153.3 million.

Q&A

  • Maurice Raycroft, Jefferies LLC: Asked about the status update for the mutant data, specifically, "if the database lock is already in place." Sullivan replied, "No, I can't comment on that."

  • Tara Bancroft, TD Cowen: Inquired about feedback from physicians and launch segmentation. Sullivan stated, "we aren't going to be targeting or narrow casting our approach to doctors or patient segments."

  • Stephen Willey, Stifel: Asked about dose escalation for prostate cancer trials and efficacy metrics. Sullivan outlined, "we're using some standard methodology to stepwise increase the dose...we're in the midst of that. So I can't really comment on where we'll end up."

  • Gil Blum, Needham & Company: Questioned potential challenges with infusions. Sullivan noted, "the IV administration shows up as a negative factor in meaningfully less than 10% of the responses we have in this research."

  • Oliver McCammon, LifeSci Capital: Asked about learnings from inavolisib's launch. Sullivan said, "the label requires fairly extensive glucose monitoring" for that therapy, and emphasized gedatolisib's advantage in not inducing hypoglycemia.

  • Eva Fortea-Verdejo, Wells Fargo: Sought updates on European commercial strategy. Sullivan described plans for MAA submission after supplemental NDA completion and ongoing regulatory engagement in Europe and Japan.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on data disclosure timelines, launch strategy, physician and payer feedback, and competitive positioning, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions sought clarification rather than expressing skepticism.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, especially when discussing clinical data and commercial outlook. Sullivan frequently referenced positive feedback, stating, "the feedback we've received, again, is very positive on that front as well."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained consistent in tone; however, management displayed increased confidence as regulatory milestones and commercial preparations advanced.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained focused on regulatory and commercial milestones, with the current quarter specifying the PDUFA date and expected timing for mutant cohort data.

  • Strategic focus shifted toward final launch preparations and commercial execution, while clinical milestone reporting timelines became more concrete.

  • The analysts' focus transitioned from clinical data and regulatory process (Q3) to commercialization, payer feedback, and competitive landscape (Q4).

  • Key financial metrics such as net loss and operating expenses increased compared to Q3, reflecting ramp-up of commercial activities and expanded headcount.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, building on strengthening market and regulatory position, with analysts' sentiment stable and focused on upcoming catalysts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the inability to discuss ongoing trial progress for the PIK3CA mutant cohort as a limitation.

  • Potential challenges with infusion administration were acknowledged but downplayed based on market research.

  • The competitive environment was addressed, with Sullivan indicating that alternative PI3K inhibitors may face biological and safety limitations compared to gedatolisib.

  • No issues were reported regarding operational runway, with cash reserves expected to finance the company through 2027.

Final Takeaway

Celcuity's leadership underscored 2025 as a transformative year, marked by the NDA acceptance for gedatolisib, priority review, and robust clinical data supporting its potential as a new standard of care in HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. The company is advancing commercial launch preparations, targeting a $5 billion market and projecting up to $2.5 billion in annual peak revenue, while maintaining strong cash reserves and a confident outlook as it approaches pivotal regulatory and clinical milestones in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Golar LNG launches strategic review, taps Goldman Sachs as advisor
data.description:
Golar LNG (GLNG) up 5.9% post-market Wednesday after saying it has initiated a formal process to evaluate strategic alternatives, including a potential sale of the company, and appointed Goldman Sachs as a financial advisor.

Golar (GLNG) said the review aims to accelerate the FLNG growth pipeline and maximize shareholder value, reflecting its "successful transition into a high-growth, pure-play floating liquefied natural gas company."

The review will include a comprehensive evaluation of the company's platform, including its FLNG technology, contract backlog, and growth pipeline, and will explore potential alternatives including a sale of the company, a merger or other business combination, divestiture of assets, or further optimization of the corporate structure.

data.title: MillerKnoll outlines Q4 net sales outlook of $955M–$995M while expanding retail footprint and managing Middle East risks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MillerKnoll, Inc. (MLKN) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Andrea Owen, President and CEO, highlighted "continued sales and order growth and disciplined execution" despite ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty and severe weather impacts. Owen emphasized the 30th anniversary of MKPS, a manufacturing system developed in partnership with Toyota, calling it "a significant competitive advantage" for efficient and high-quality production.
  • Owen stated, "We expect to continue to fully offset tariff costs for the remainder of this fiscal year as we did in the third quarter." She also noted disruption in the Middle East, saying, "we do expect some impact to fourth quarter sales and costs."
  • North America Contract showed "order growth in most sectors" and Owen expressed optimism about demand resiliency. Owen discussed upcoming launches at the NeoCon trade show, including products from Herman Miller, Knoll, Geiger, NaughtOne, HAY, Muuto, and Maharam.
  • In Global Retail, Owen reported "segment comparable sales increased 5.5%" with North America up 3.9%, despite adverse weather. The company opened new DWR locations in Fort Worth and Pittsburgh and a Herman Miller store in Phoenix, planning to open 3 to 4 more locations before fiscal year-end, aiming to "approximately double our DWR Herman Miller store footprint over the next several years."
  • Owen welcomed Claire Spofford, former CEO of J. Jill, to the Board, citing her "consumer insight, retail strategy and governance experience."
  • CFO Kevin Veltman stated, "In the third quarter, we generated adjusted earnings per share of $0.43 compared to $0.44 in the same quarter last year... Consolidated net sales for the quarter were $927 million, up 5.8% year-over-year on a reported basis and 3.8% higher organically."
  • Veltman reported, "We generated $61 million in cash flow from operations in the quarter and reduced our debt by $41 million, lowering our debt-to-EBITDA ratio to 2.75x as defined by our lending agreement."

Outlook

  • Veltman provided Q4 guidance: "We expect net sales to range between $955 million and $995 million, up 1.4% versus prior year, at the midpoint of $975 million."
  • "Gross margin is projected to be between 37.5% and 38.5% and includes higher expected logistics costs from higher oil prices related to the conflict in the Middle East."
  • Adjusted diluted earnings are expected to range between $0.49 and $0.55 per share, including "our current estimate that the direct impact of the Middle East conflict will be $8 million to $9 million in the quarter or $0.09 to $0.10 per share."
  • Incremental year-over-year operating expense for new store locations in global retail is expected at $3.5 million to $4.5 million.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated orders grew to $932 million, up 9.2% as reported.
  • The consolidated backlog was $712 million at quarter end.
  • North America Contract net sales were $489 million; orders increased to $491 million. Operating margin for the segment was 8.6% and adjusted margin was 9.8%.
  • International Contract net sales reached $157 million. Orders were $160 million, with adjusted operating margin at 8.2%.
  • Global Retail net sales were $281 million, with a segment operating margin of 2.2% and adjusted margin of 2.8%.
  • Dividend declared at $0.1875 per share, payable April 15.

Q&A

  • Douglas Lane, Water Tower Research LLC, asked about weather impact. Owen responded, "We definitely saw lower traffic than normal across our retail stores. We had quite a few closures during that frigid weather period."
  • Lane questioned cost pressures from the Middle East. Owen replied, "We haven't seen much except for increase in diesel and things that are really impacting oil-related fuel so far, but we anticipate we'll see increase in cost of plastics, foam..."
  • Lane inquired about inventory strategy. Owen stated, "We have dual supply in a variety of places for most of our really important components... so far, not yet."
  • Lane asked about office environment trends. Owen said, "We continue to see momentum. We continue to see architectural billings moving in the right direction."
  • Olivia May Witte, William Blair, questioned contract and retail volatility. Owen explained, "We have built in some caution around oil prices... so far, we have a consumer that tends to be premium and tends to be rather unaffected by many of these changes."
  • Witte also asked about government contract trends. John Michael, President of North America Contract, said, "We came into this year expecting that the federal government business would be rather tough... but there's still activity there."
  • Reuben Garner, The Benchmark Company, clarified the Middle East impact. Veltman responded, "The sales that we don't expect to be able to ship in the quarter is pretty close to what our run rate has been, that $12 million that I mentioned."
  • Garner asked about AI and tech sector demand. Michael stated, "the tech sector is very active right now, particularly in the Bay Area."
  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company, asked about store opening pace. Veltman replied, "we're expecting next year's store openings to be in a similar zone to the 14 to 15 this year, maybe a touch higher."
  • Burns asked about retail gross margin. Propst explained, "we had a favorable freight true-up last year" and "incremental ship revenue costs in Q3 as we pushed into some free shipping promos."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on macro risks, cost pressures, and retail momentum with a slightly cautious tone, highlighted by repeated questions on Middle East impacts and consumer demand.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive stance, repeatedly emphasizing scenario planning and flexibility. Owen used phrases such as "we anticipate," "we are very experienced in navigating tariff changes," and "we continue to be optimistic."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone in prepared remarks was similarly confident, but Q&A responses reflected more caution regarding global risks and cost inflation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 guidance incorporated explicit headwinds from the Middle East conflict, while Q2 focused more on tariff risks and new store investments.
  • Strategic focus shifted from primarily execution and expansion to also include risk mitigation and supply chain agility.
  • Analysts in both quarters queried about macro impacts, but Q3 saw heightened concern on geopolitical and oil-related risks.
  • Gross margin projections narrowed slightly, reflecting expected logistics and commodity pressures.
  • Management's confidence in retail expansion and contract order growth remained steady, but there was increased emphasis on scenario planning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, especially the Middle East conflict, is expected to impact Q4 sales and costs.
  • Management flagged potential cost increases in oil, logistics, plastics, and foam, and is monitoring these developments daily.
  • Severe weather disrupted retail traffic and operations.
  • The government contract business is "rather tough" with expectations for choppiness in coming months.

Final Takeaway

MillerKnoll delivered a solid quarter, demonstrating resilience in sales and order growth despite challenging external conditions. Management anticipates continued headwinds from the Middle East conflict and higher oil-related costs but remains committed to expanding its retail footprint, leveraging its manufacturing efficiency, and pursuing growth in both contract and retail segments. The company is closely monitoring cost pressures and stands ready to adjust pricing and supply strategies as needed, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dolphin Entertainment targets accelerated margin expansion and cash flow growth with DealMaker and AI initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dolphin Entertainment (DLPN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William O'Dowd highlighted that 2025 was "the first without a major acquisition," emphasizing the company's transition from an acquisition-driven strategy to focusing on organic growth and operational leverage. O'Dowd reported, "Full year revenue grew approximately 10% to $56.7 million. Fourth quarter revenue was $15.6 million, up 27% year-over-year." He stressed that this growth was "entirely organic" and pointed to a significant acceleration in profitability and cash flow. "Full year adjusted EBITDA reached $2.9 million, which is up over 200% from $900,000 in 2024." O'Dowd underscored the company's capital-light model, noting "Dolphin requires very little capital expenditure to operate...when we generate incremental EBITDA, that incremental EBITDA translates almost directly into free cash flow." He cited $127 million in net operating loss carryforwards as providing substantial tax shielding.
  • On strategic initiatives, O'Dowd described the "strategic partnership with DealMaker" as "one of the most exciting developments in Dolphin's history and a meaningful growth catalyst for us," targeting celebrity and influencer-led capital raises with minimal capital outlay.
  • O'Dowd also introduced "Dolphin Intelligence," a new division focused on AI-driven marketing, which "offers a suite of new services, including generative engine optimization and AI engine optimization strategy." The division is led by Mark Anderson and is positioned as both a growth driver and an internal efficiency tool.
  • CFO Mirta Negrini stated, "Total revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $56.7 million, an increase of 10% from $51.7 million in the prior year." Negrini reported, "Operating loss was $39,058 for the year ended December 31, 2025, compared to an operating loss of $10.5 million for the year ended December 31, 2024." She added, "Net loss for 2025 was approximately $3.1 million...This compares to a net loss of $12.6 million in 2024."

Outlook

  • O'Dowd projected "continued revenue growth in 2026" with contributions from both "dealmaker-related marketing engagements and Dolphin Intelligence services as those ramp in the second half of the year." He stated, "We expect adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to continue. At 5% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2025, we believe we are just getting started. The infrastructure is built and incremental revenue carries high flow-through." He also reiterated the expectation that "adjusted EBITDA to grow significantly faster than revenue again in 2026."
  • O'Dowd provided visibility on cash flow drivers: "approximately $1 million in expected annualized lease savings beginning at the end of this year when our current New York leases roll off. Los Angeles ends at the end of next year in 2027." He also pointed to "full repayment of our bank debt within approximately 2.5 years, September 29, 2028."

Financial Results

  • Dolphin reported full year revenue of $56.7 million and Q4 revenue of $15.6 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year was $2.9 million, with Q4 adjusted EBITDA at $1.7 million, compared to an adjusted EBITDA loss of $0.5 million in Q4 2024. Operating expenses for 2025 were $56.7 million, including $2.4 million of depreciation and amortization. Net loss for 2025 was $3.1 million, down from $12.6 million in 2024. Basic and diluted loss per share for the year was $0.27.
  • The company highlighted $127 million in federal and state NOLs as a major factor expected to shield cash taxes and support free cash flow conversion.

Q&A

  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group LLC: "I wanted to ask about just the DealMaker partnership you had outlined...if you could get a little more granular in terms of how that's tied directly to deal flow and how those opportunities will be sourced?" O'Dowd responded that DealMaker allows Dolphin "to scale a massive opportunity...to launch consumer products with our own clients" and described anticipated marketing campaigns as "in the 6 figures per year per brand." He noted a "very strong and robust pipeline" from both Hollywood partners and existing clients.
  • Greenberg: "Generally, what do you expect the length of deals to be? And who's typically the investors...?" O'Dowd explained, "Reg CF allows you to raise up to $5 million per raise every 12 months...we would look to complete the raise in full within 4 months typically." He added, "the typical investment size is probably $1,000 to $2,000" per investor, highlighting the community-driven nature of the offerings.
  • Greenberg: "Just your venture portfolio...timing on your end in terms of when you think to add additional ventures...?" O'Dowd stated, "We had given ourselves a 60-day window...I would hope to be in market with the first one this summer."
  • Greenberg: "AI and Intelligence segment...how you expect that to contribute to growth...?" O'Dowd detailed that existing clients are expected to be early adopters and that Dolphin Intelligence is also "a business development tool for us" to attract new, larger clients.
  • Greenberg: "Youngblood...biggest opportunity is up ahead with selling the streaming rights. How is that process going...?" O'Dowd said, "we have a second window...which is what we call electronic sell-through or pay-per-view...That window is opening up here at the end of the month of March."
  • Greenberg: "Overall performance this year, double-digit organic growth. Do you think that level is sustainable going forward?" O'Dowd replied, "I do believe we're going to grow every year just organically like this...any incremental revenue growth should have an outsized importance on the margin expansion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a positive and inquisitive tone, expressing interest in the specifics of the DealMaker partnership, Dolphin Intelligence, and the sustainability of growth, as seen in questions like, "Do you think that level is sustainable going forward?"
  • Management's tone was confident and focused, using phrases such as "we are just getting started" and "we believe...2026 will continue the beginning of that inflection." There was a consistent emphasis on operational leverage and strategic opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence level remained high, but the current call featured more forward-looking detail about new business lines and specific cash flow catalysts. Analysts' tone shifted from congratulatory (Q3) to more probing about growth drivers and execution details in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language in Q4 emphasized continued revenue growth and expanding EBITDA margins, building on the Q3 narrative of strong organic growth and operational simplification.
  • Strategic focus shifted in Q4 to highlight the launch of Dolphin Intelligence and the DealMaker partnership, whereas Q3 focused more on agency cross-selling and operational results.
  • Analysts in Q4 directed more of their questions toward new business development and monetization of strategic initiatives, reflecting heightened interest in forward-looking catalysts.
  • Management’s tone in both quarters was highly confident, but Q4 included more detailed discussion of pipeline development, margin expansion, and cash flow optimization.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and net loss showed marked improvement in Q4 versus Q3, and there was a greater emphasis on the expected acceleration of free cash flow in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • O'Dowd acknowledged that "our business has seasonality to it. Historically, our first quarter tends to be our lightest with revenue building through the year so that the fourth quarter typically becomes our strongest."
  • The CEO flagged that the theatrical window for Youngblood "may have underperformed, but we are straightforward about that. The real opportunity has always been in the streaming and digital distribution tail."
  • Management stressed a disciplined, capital-light approach to new ventures, minimizing financial risk: "We contribute our capabilities rather than our capital, and we look for opportunities with asymmetric upside."

Final Takeaway

Dolphin Entertainment’s management presented a narrative of accelerating organic growth, margin expansion, and a capital-light business model backed by significant tax assets. The company is leveraging new strategic partnerships, particularly with DealMaker and the launch of Dolphin Intelligence, to expand its addressable market and drive recurring, high-margin revenue streams. Management conveyed confidence in further EBITDA and cash flow growth through 2026, supported by cost-saving catalysts and operational scale, while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk and capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
